---
title: "Teaching"
pagetitle: "Teaching — Bingjin Xue"
---

## Current courses at University of New Hampshire

::: {.course}
### ECON 898 / 720 — Machine Learning for Economists
<div class="term">Graduate · Spring 2024 – present</div>

<details class="course-body">
<summary>Description</summary>

Bridges economic reasoning with modern machine learning. Students learn to build, test, and interpret models in Python — from regularized regression to causal forests — grounded in economic theory. The goal is not to treat machine learning as a black box, but to train economists to engineer algorithms with purpose and interpretability for research and policy evaluation.

</details>

[Syllabus](assets/ECON898-S26-Xue.pdf){target="_blank" aria-label="Syllabus PDF"}
:::

::: {.course}
### ECON 927 — Econometrics II
<div class="term">Graduate · Spring 2024 – present</div>

<details class="course-body">
<summary>Description</summary>

Second graduate course in econometrics, with a focus on identification and inference. Starts with three preliminaries — linear algebra, statistics, and asymptotic theory — then covers OLS, GLS, MLE, GMM, IV, and modern difference-in-differences (including TWFE). Substantial time on endogeneity and the design choices that make causal estimates credible.

</details>

[Syllabus](assets/ECON927-S25-Xue.pdf){target="_blank" aria-label="Syllabus PDF"}
:::

::: {.course}
### ECON 565 — Predictive Modeling
<div class="term">Undergraduate · Spring 2026 – present</div>

<details class="course-body">
<summary>Description</summary>

Undergraduate course on turning data into decisions. Students develop statistical programming skills — collecting, cleaning, analyzing, and presenting data — and build predictive models. The economics emphasis is on using data to sharpen marginal-analysis decisions in business and policy.

</details>

[Syllabus](assets/ECON565-S26-Xue.pdf){target="_blank" aria-label="Syllabus PDF"}
:::

::: {.course}
### ECON 402 — Principles of Microeconomics
<div class="term">Undergraduate · Fall 2023 – present</div>

<details class="course-body">
<summary>Description</summary>

Introductory microeconomics. How individuals, firms, and societies make choices under scarcity. We cover markets, prices, resource allocation, market failures, firm behavior, and competitive environments — leaving students with a framework for thinking about how to make the best use of resources, technology, and skills.

</details>

[Syllabus](assets/ECON402-F25-Xue.pdf){target="_blank" aria-label="Syllabus PDF"}
:::
